quick_facts.label: What it is
quick_facts.value: NEET-MDS is the national eligibility-cum-ranking examination and single entrance examination for admission to MDS courses in India; it is conducted by NBEMS.

quick_facts.label: 2026 examination date
quick_facts.value: For the 2026 admission session, NBEMS conducted NEET-MDS on May 2, 2026, as a computer-based examination at centres across India.

quick_facts.label: Core eligibility
quick_facts.value: For the 2026 cycle, candidates needed a recognised BDS degree, State Dental Council registration (provisional or permanent), and a one-year compulsory rotatory internship at an approved/recognised dental college. The internship completion cut-off was May 31, 2026.

quick_facts.label: 2026 exam format
quick_facts.value: The 2026 paper had 240 English-language multiple-choice questions, four response options per question, and three hours of testing time. It was divided into time-bound Part A (100 questions, 75 minutes) and Part B (140 questions, 105 minutes).

quick_facts.label: 2026 qualifying benchmark
quick_facts.value: For NEET-MDS 2026, NBEMS reported qualifying cut-offs of 308 for General/EWS at the 50th percentile, 289 for General PwBD at the 45th percentile, and 271 for SC/ST/OBC including PwBD at the 40th percentile, out of 960. These are dated, session-specific thresholds and may change in later cycles.

quick_facts.label: Counselling responsibility
quick_facts.value: NBEMS conducts the exam, declares results, and supplies results to the designated counselling authority; it does not conduct MDS seat counselling or allotment. MCC/DGHS conducts counselling for the categories of seats within its remit, while States/UTs apply their own rules for state-quota admissions.

sections.heading: Purpose and place in the dental education pathway
paragraphs: NEET-MDS is the postgraduate entrance examination following BDS for admission to Master of Dental Surgery (MDS) programmes. For the 2026 session, the bulletin described it as the single eligibility-cum-ranking examination for MDS admissions under the Dentists Act, 1948; state- or institution-level entrance tests were not valid for MDS admission.

Its 2026 scope included 50% All India Quota MDS seats, state-quota seats, private dental colleges and universities, and Armed Forces Medical Services MDS courses. AIIMS New Delhi was listed as outside NEET-MDS coverage for that session.

Passing or appearing in NEET-MDS does not itself guarantee a seat. Admission remains subject to applicable institutional, university, counselling, medical-fitness, reservation, and documentary requirements.
bullets: Use NEET-MDS to establish eligibility and merit for MDS admission after BDS.
Treat the examination rank as one part of admission: counselling registration, choices, allotment, and document verification are separate stages.
Check the relevant counselling authority and institute rules for the seat category you intend to pursue.

sections.heading: Eligibility after BDS
paragraphs: For NEET-MDS 2026, the baseline requirement was a recognised BDS degree from an Indian university or institution, registration with a State Dental Council, and provisional or permanent registration plus one year of compulsory rotatory internship in an approved or recognised dental college.

A candidate who was still completing the 12-month internship could take the 2026 examination if likely to complete it by May 31, 2026. However, admission required completion of the internship and registration by that cut-off, or the specified certification from the head of institution.

Eligibility is provisional at examination stage and is checked again by the counselling or admitting authority. State quota, domicile, institutional, reservation, and other admission conditions can differ by State or institution.
bullets: Confirm that the BDS qualification is recognised for the applicable admission cycle.
Track the internship-completion cut-off in the current information bulletin; the May 31, 2026 date applied only to the 2026 cycle.
Keep original registration and internship-completion evidence available for counselling and admission verification.

sections.heading: Examination format and scoring
paragraphs: In 2026, NEET-MDS was a single-day, single-session computer-based test. The syllabus was at BDS standard and covered subjects taught in the BDS curriculum under the DCI Revised BDS Course Regulations, 2007, as amended.

The 2026 paper contained 240 English-language MCQs with four options each. Part A contained 100 questions in 75 minutes and Part B contained 140 questions in 105 minutes. Candidates could not move to the next part early; when a part closed, they could not revisit or change its responses.

The 2026 marking scheme awarded four marks for a correct answer, deducted one mark for an incorrect answer, and awarded zero for an unattempted question. These operational details should be checked again against the bulletin for the intended exam year.
bullets: Plan preparation around BDS subjects rather than assuming a narrow specialty-only syllabus.
Practise under sectional time limits, not only under a three-hour full-test limit.
Account for negative marking when developing an attempt strategy.

sections.heading: Application and examination process
paragraphs: For the 2026 cycle, NBEMS made the information bulletin available on March 13, 2026, opened online applications from March 14, 2026 at 3:00 PM, and closed them on March 30, 2026 at 11:55 PM. These dates are historical and should not be used as a schedule for a future cycle.

The 2026 application fee was ₹3,500 for General, OBC, and EWS candidates and ₹2,500 for SC, ST, and PwD candidates, excluding applicable payment-gateway charges. Fees and categories are session-specific and may change. NBEMS required online payment through the application payment gateway.

After the examination, candidates use their score and rank in the applicable counselling system. For 2026, NBEMS said that counselling registration would use details supplied in the NEET-MDS application, so candidates were advised to retain the same mobile number and email address.
bullets: Use only the NBEMS NEET-MDS examination page and current information bulletin when an application window is announced.
Submit accurate academic, internship, registration, identity, category, and contact details; eligibility and documents are verified later.
Preserve the admit card, scorecard, payment confirmation, BDS records, registration documents, and internship-completion certificate for counselling.

sections.heading: Preparation approach grounded in the official scheme
paragraphs: Start from the official BDS-standard syllabus statement and build a subject-wise revision plan covering the full BDS curriculum. Because the 2026 examination used 240 MCQs in three hours, preparation should combine content revision with sustained question practice.

Make timed mocks reflect the actual two-part structure: 75 minutes for 100 questions, then 105 minutes for 140 questions. The 2026 rules prevented revisiting a completed part, so practising a strict section-close routine is useful.

Use error review to improve both knowledge and decision-making. With +4 for a correct answer and −1 for an incorrect answer in 2026, review why answers were wrong, whether the error was factual or interpretive, and whether an uncertain attempt was justified.
bullets: Create a BDS-subject checklist from the current bulletin and track first-pass revision, question practice, and error revision.
Take computer-based, timed practice tests using the current year's section timing where available.
Re-read the current official bulletin before the exam for test-day documents, reporting time, prohibited items, and identity-verification rules.

sections.heading: Results, rank, and progression to MDS admission
paragraphs: NEET-MDS produces an examination score and national rank used in the MDS admission process. For 2026, NBEMS declared results on June 2, 2026 and made individual scorecards available from June 10, 2026; scorecards were available for download for six months. These dates and access periods are specific to that cycle.

The NEET-MDS 2026 result was valid only for the 2026 admission session. NBEMS stated that the final merit/category lists for state-quota seats would be generated by the respective States and UTs under their own eligibility criteria, regulations, and reservation policies.

For central counselling categories, MCC/DGHS is responsible for the online counselling process for 50% All India Quota postgraduate dental seats and other listed institutional categories. Candidates should therefore follow both NBEMS for examination notices and the relevant counselling authority for counselling schedules, seat matrices, choices, allotment, and joining requirements.
bullets: Download and retain the scorecard promptly when released.
Use the applicable counselling portal rather than assuming NBEMS will allot a seat.
Verify state-specific eligibility, domicile, reservation, and institutional rules before relying on a NEET-MDS rank for a particular seat pool.
